APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Yes — the paper addresses multimodal grounding and referring expression generation, a clear fit for top NLP/ML venues (e.g. ACL/EMNLP/NeurIPS) by combining distributional semantics and visual features. It targets the right audience: it cites and builds on relevant multimodal and cross-modal work (Lazaridou et al., Frome et al., Norouzi et al.) and REG literature (Kazemzadeh et al., Schlangen et al.). The problem formulation (object naming / head-noun prediction on the REFERIT corpus) and experimental setup (GoogleNet visual features, word2vec embeddings, hit@k metrics, zero-shot evaluations) match community standards. The paper is well positioned within the multimodal NLP/vision research community and uses accepted methods and evaluations for this venue.

IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles a meaningful problem — generating appropriate head nouns for referring expressions in real images — using a realistic REFERIT corpus and modern visual/text embeddings. By comparing and combining cross-modal mapping (TRANSFER), visual word classifiers (WAC) and the novel SIM-WAP (which lets predictors be trained for words without visual exemplars) it demonstrates complementary strengths, improved naming accuracy (Table 2), and promising zero-shot behavior, especially for singular/plural cases. These findings clarify when distributional information helps vs. hurts reference and suggest concrete directions for integrating lexical semantics into REG and zero-shot labeling, making the work likely influential across vision–language and referring-expression research.

CLARITY.justification: The paper contains a clear high-level structure (Task/Data → Models → Experiments → Zero-shot) but the provided text is marred by heavy repetition, duplicated paragraphs and stray LaTeX/citation artifacts (e.g. many repeated lines around Section 2–4, multiple copies of the same sentences), which breaks the logical flow and makes reading difficult. Main contributions are implied through experiments but are not cleanly stated up front (no concise contribution paragraph), and some notation/terms (TRANSFER, SIM‑WAP, WAC, v/w) are used informally though understandable. Tables are present and informative (Table 2/3), but referenced figures (e.g. Figure 1) are missing and several sentences are garbled ("(Anonymous)", repeated citations), indicating significant copyediting and organization problems that need fixing.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper is an incremental but coherent combination of existing ideas (cross-modal transfer à la Lazaridou/Frome, visual word classifiers/WAC) applied to the task of naming head nouns in referring expressions. Its most novel technical contribution is the SIM‑WAP extension that trains per‑word regressors on continuous similarity labels and explicitly shows how to train predictors for words lacking visual instances (“going beyond (Anonymous)”), plus empirical exploration of model aggregation and zero‑shot splits (hypernym / singular–plural). Overall the methods are a non‑trivial engineering/empirical extension of prior work rather than a highly original theoretical advance, and the paper clearly positions itself relative to prior cross‑modal and REG work.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper runs multiple complementary experiments (standard naming, model combinations shown in Table 2, cosine analyses in Table 3, and detailed zero-shot evaluations under both full-vocabulary and disjoint-vocabulary regimes, including hypernym and singular/plural tests), and discusses complementary strengths/weaknesses of TRANSFER, WAC and SIM-WAP. It lacks explicit ablation or sensitivity analyses for hyperparameters, feature variants or training choices (only Ridge regression is mentioned without regularization/training details), and provides only limited quantitative error analysis beyond aggregate metrics. Related work is covered broadly and limitations (e.g. distributional vs referential mismatch, naming nuances) are acknowledged. Reproducibility is partially supported (dataset, embeddings, feature extractors and model types specified) but important implementation/training details are missing.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear claims (TRANSFER vs. WAC vs. SIM‑WAP behavior, complementariness and zero‑shot strengths) and presents experimental evidence (e.g. Table 2 and the zero‑shot comparisons) supporting them. The mathematical content is minimal (ridge regression, cosine similarity) and appears correct. The experimental setup uses a standard corpus and reasonable baselines, but important methodological details are missing (e.g. regularization/hyperparameter selection, class imbalance handling, exact train/test splits, and no statistical significance testing), and the reduced vocabulary/filtered REs limit generality. These are minor issues that do not overturn the reported findings, so the paper is technically sound apart from those omitted experimental details.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines (e.g. Lazaridou et al. 2014/2015, Frome et al. 2013, Norouzi et al. 2013, Socher et al., Schlangen et al. 2016, Ordonez et al. 2016) and cites relevant multimodal work (Kiela, Kottur, etc.). Comparisons appear fair: models are evaluated on the same REFERIT data (using Schlangen et al.’s train/test split), with standard metrics (hit@k, cosine distances) and zero-shot setups that follow Lazaridou/Norouzi conventions. The paper explicitly acknowledges prior findings (e.g. transfer approaches degrading flat accuracy, Lazaridou’s observations) and analyzes differences (taxonomic bias of TRANSFER, instance sensitivity of WAC, SIM‑WAP’s zero-shot strengths), including complementary behavior and model combination gains. Overall the coverage and analysis of competing approaches are comprehensive and appropriate.
